items.q: What do Christians mean by resurrection, and how does it differ from reincarnation?
items.correct: Resurrection is the once-for-all bodily raising to eternal life at the end of time (modelled on Christ's resurrection); reincarnation is the soul's repeated rebirth into new bodies. They are distinct doctrines from different religious traditions.
items.misconception: Resurrection and reincarnation are basically the same idea of life after death.
items.random_wrong: Christians believe everyone is reincarnated until they accept Jesus.
items.why: Christianity affirms one bodily resurrection, not cyclical rebirth. Students flatten different afterlife concepts into a single 'life after death' idea, missing the very different metaphysics involved.

items.correct: The absolute oneness and indivisibility of Allah — the most fundamental Islamic belief. Its opposite, shirk (associating partners with God), is the gravest sin.
items.misconception: Tawhid is the Islamic word for the Trinity.
items.random_wrong: Tawhid is the name of an Islamic prayer position.
items.why: Tawhid is the strict denial of any plurality in God — directly contrasting with Trinitarian Christianity. Students sometimes invert the meaning entirely. The Quran's emphasis on Tawhid shapes Islamic theology, art (no images) and ethics.

items.q: What are the main Christian theories of atonement?
items.correct: Penal substitution (Christ bore the punishment for sin), Christus Victor (Christ defeated evil powers through the cross), ransom theory (Christ paid a price to free humanity), and moral exemplar (Christ's death inspires love). Different traditions emphasise different theories.
items.misconception: All Christians believe Jesus died only to pay God back for sin.
items.random_wrong: Atonement means Jesus apologised to humans for being born divine.
items.why: Penal substitution dominates Protestant evangelicalism but isn't the only or oldest model. Christus Victor was prevalent in the early church and Orthodox traditions still emphasise it. Students often treat one model as 'the' Christian view of atonement.

items.q: When was the Trinity affirmed by council?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: AD 325. “AD 33” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who developed the doctrine of original sin?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Augustine. “Aquinas” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: On what day did Jesus rise according to Christians?
items.why: The real figure is 3 day. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
